planting.note: Divide established crowns in early spring or plant container-grown plants in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in shade to part shade; cinnamon fern is a wetland and streamside plant that tolerates standing water briefly but prefers reliably moist, never-dry conditions. It grows naturally in swamps, bogs, moist woods, and streambanks and is the most heat-tolerant of the osmunda ferns.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in full to part shade; cinnamon fern is one of the most beautiful and distinctive native ferns, providing a remarkable two-season display: in spring, the emerging croziers (fiddleheads) are densely covered with cinnamon-brown wooly hairs — giving the fern its name — and the separate, upright, cinnamon-colored fertile fronds that bear the spores arise from the center of the clump like brown candles, producing a distinctive spring silhouette. The fertile fronds are produced before the vegetative fronds and persist through the growing season, turning from green to rich, warm brown as the spores ripen. The vegetative fronds are large, arching, and medium-green, forming vase-shaped clumps of considerable scale and presence in the shade garden. Native throughout eastern North America from Canada to Florida, it is the most heat-tolerant of the large osmunda ferns and grows in zone 10 in Florida's swamps. The woolly fiddlehead hairs have historically been harvested as potting medium for orchids. Forms long-lived, increasingly large crowns that are landmarks in moist shaded gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; common myrtle is drought-tolerant once established and native to the dry Mediterranean basin. It performs well in zone 8–10 gardens with dry summers, is sensitive to prolonged hard freezes below 10°F, and does best in sheltered positions in zone 8.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; common myrtle is one of the oldest cultivated plants in human history — sacred to Aphrodite, woven into Roman bridal wreaths, and mentioned in the Old Testament — a Mediterranean evergreen shrub with small, intensely aromatic leaves (releasing a fresh, clean, camphor-like fragrance when brushed), white flowers with prominent yellow stamens in summer, and small, round, blue-black berries in autumn. All parts of the plant are aromatic and culinary: the leaves are used to flavor meats and poultry in Sardinian and Corsican cuisine (similar to bay leaves), the berries are edible with a resinous, slightly gin-like flavor and are used to flavor liqueurs and as a spice in traditional Mediterranean cooking. The plant is compact and shapeable into hedges, topiary, and formal geometric shapes. Both leaves and berries are used in myrtle liqueur, particularly the Sardinian Mirto. It grows well in zone 8–10 with dry summers and is an excellent plant for Gulf Coast and California gardens with mild winters.

planting.note: Direct-sow after last frost in moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Job's tears grows as a perennial in zones 9–11 and as an annual in cooler climates. It tolerates partial shade and moist to wet soils — an unusual quality among grain crops — and can be grown at water's edge. The hard, bead-like grains are surrounded by glossy white or gray pseudo-involucres that can be strung as beads.

directions: Direct-sow in moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Job's tears is a large, tropical grass from Southeast Asia grown both as a grain crop and as an ornamental and craft plant. The plant is unmistakable: large, corn-like leaves on tall stems produce pendulous clusters of hard, rounded, bead-like structures (involucres) containing the true grain, which ripen from green to white, gray, and sometimes blue-gray or black. These natural beads have a hole through the center and have been used for necklaces, rosaries, and ceremonial jewelry for thousands of years across Asia and the Pacific. The grain itself (adlay) is nutritious, with a mild, earthy flavor resembling barley, and is used in soups, porridges, teas, and brewing in China, Korea, Japan, and Southeast Asia. It is considered medicinal in traditional Chinese medicine. In zone 9–11, the plant can grow as a short-lived perennial. It tolerates moist soil and partial shade better than most grain crops, making it suitable for wet areas of the garden.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center

planting.note: Direct-sow after soil is thoroughly warm (above 60°F) in spring or early summer; sudangrass grows very rapidly in hot conditions and should be mowed or tilled when it reaches 18–24 inches to prevent it from setting seed and to stimulate branching. For summer cover crop use in hot climates like the Gulf Coast, it is ideal — it germinates and establishes in June heat that kills cool-season cover crops.

directions: Direct-sow in warm, well-drained soil after soil temperatures exceed 60°F; sudangrass is the premier warm-season summer cover crop for the South and Gulf Coast, growing rapidly in the intense summer heat that kills cool-season cover crops like crimson clover and winter rye — it can gain 6–8 inches per week in hot conditions. The dense root system breaks up compacted soil, and the rapidly decomposing residue adds significant organic matter. Mow when 18–24 inches tall (before heading) to encourage dense regrowth and prevent seed set. It produces allelopathic compounds that suppress some weed seeds — incorporate into the soil 2–4 weeks before planting vegetables to allow the allelopathic effect to dissipate. Sudangrass x sorghum hybrids ('BMR' or 'brown midrib' types) break down faster in the soil than standard varieties and are preferred where rapid residue decomposition is needed. An outstanding solution for the summer fallow period in zone 7–11 vegetable gardens where nothing else grows quickly enough in the heat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; achachairu is more cold-tolerant than mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana) and reportedly survives brief dips to 28–30°F when established. Young trees are more sensitive to cold than mature ones — protect for the first several years. It is commercially grown in Bolivia at elevations up to 3,000 feet.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; achachairu is a Bolivian fruit tree in the same genus as mangosteen (Garcinia) and widely considered the superior subtropical Garcinia for warm temperate gardens — significantly more cold-tolerant than true mangosteen (which requires tropical conditions) while producing excellent fruit. The oval, orange-yellow fruits are 2–3 inches long with a thick, hard rind and sections of white, juicy, translucent flesh with a flavor profile that directly recalls mangosteen: sweet, tropical, slightly acidic, and complex, with an aromatic quality unique in temperate fruits. The tree is dense, dark-green, and attractive as an ornamental, with glossy leaves and a naturally compact, rounded form. Commercially grown in Bolivia, the fruit is popular fresh and in juices, ice creams, and desserts. It grows much faster than mangosteen and begins fruiting in 7–10 years from seed. Australia has embraced it as a commercial orchard crop, and it is increasingly available in subtropical zones 9–11 in the United States.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit
